When I started learning React Native, I was genuinely excited.
Coming from a web development background, I thought it would be a smooth transition. After all, it’s still React, just for mobile — right?
But a few days into building my first real app, I hit a point where I seriously considered stopping.
Not because React Native is bad.
But because I underestimated what mobile development actually involves.
The excitement phase
At the beginning, everything felt great.
I could reuse my JavaScript knowledge, build components quickly, and see results on my phone almost instantly using Expo.
For a moment, it felt like I had “cracked” mobile development.
I was moving fast. Too fast.
When things started breaking
The problems didn’t appear all at once. They slowly stacked up.
At first, it was small things:

• Layouts looking different on real devices

• Buttons not behaving as expected

• Weird spacing issues on smaller screens
Then it got more frustrating:

• Debugging errors that didn’t make sense

• Issues that only appeared on Android but not iOS

• Performance drops I couldn’t easily explain
The same app that looked perfect in the emulator suddenly felt unreliable on real devices.
That was the first moment I thought:
“Maybe I’m not ready for this.”
The breaking point
The real low point came when I tried to prepare the app for a proper build.
What I thought would be a “final step” turned into a whole new world of problems:

• Build errors

• Configuration issues

• Platform-specific bugs

• Confusing documentation differences
At that point, I stopped feeling like I was building an app.
I felt like I was constantly fixing things I didn’t fully understand.
That’s when I seriously considered giving up.
Not on programming — but on React Native specifically.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What saved me
The turning point wasn’t a single tutorial or magic fix.
It was a mindset shift.
I realized I was trying to understand everything before moving forward.
So I changed my approach:
1. I stopped aiming for perfection
Instead of trying to build a perfect app, I focused on building a working version first.
2. I broke everything into small problems
Instead of thinking “the app is broken,” I started asking:

• What exactly is broken?

• Where does it happen?

• Can I isolate it?
This made problems feel manageable.
3. I accepted that mobile development is different
This was important.
React Native is familiar, but mobile is still mobile.
Device testing, builds, and platform differences are part of the process — not edge cases.
4. Expo became my safety net
Using Expo helped me reduce friction.
It didn’t solve all problems, but it removed a lot of setup complexity and let me focus on actually building.
That alone kept me going.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What I learned from this experience
Looking back, I didn’t fail.
I just hit the normal wall every developer hits when moving into mobile development.
The important lessons were:

• Feeling stuck is part of the process

• Real device testing matters more than emulators

• Shipping is harder than building

• Progress matters more than perfection
Most importantly, I learned that the moment you feel like giving up is usually the moment right before you actually start improving.
Final thoughts
React Native didn’t become easier overnight.
But I became more comfortable with uncertainty, debugging, and building through problems instead of waiting to fully understand everything first.
If you’re in that same phase — where everything feels harder than expected — you’re probably not doing anything wrong.
You’re just in the middle of the learning curve.
And that’s exactly where real progress starts.
